lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market contractors installing ceramic tile, interior stone, mosaic, and job-site terrazzo for external residential and commercial customers, including new construction, renovation, maintenance, and repair work supported by repeat relationships with general contractors, builders, designers, property operators, or recurring customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Exterior stone masonry, resilient-flooring installation, retail sale-and-install operations classified outside this NAICS, precast-terrazzo manufacturing, captive internal crews, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms without a repeatable outsourced-service customer base.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Project revenue from property owners and, frequently, subcontract work for general contractors or builders; jobs are commonly bid from plans, measurements, labor, and material requirements, with repeat business driven by trade relationships rather than subscription contracts.

a.bridge: BLS measures the occupation mix of the broader NAICS 238300 Building Finishing Contractors, not NAICS 238340. I converted major-group employment shares and mean wages into approximate wage weights, then judged task exposure using the O*NET tile-setter task list: estimating, blueprint interpretation, ordering, scheduling, documentation, sales, and office work are partly AI-exposed, while cutting, setting, grouting, surface preparation, and material handling are not. The broader-industry proxy likely biases exposure upward because it includes larger and more administratively layered finishing trades; omission of self-employed owners partly offsets that direction.
a.rationale: In the BLS parent-industry mix, construction occupations are 70.96% of employment, while office support is 8.59%, management 5.32%, business and financial operations 4.63%, and sales 2.94% (S2). O*NET shows the core occupation is dominated by physical alignment, cutting, mortar application, surface preparation, and installation, alongside a smaller set of information tasks such as blueprint study, estimates, ordering, and customer selection (S3). A wage-weighted task allocation therefore supports a modest exposed share concentrated outside the installation itself.
a.change_evidence: A custom OEWS staffing-and-wage extract at six-digit NAICS 238340 combined with a time-and-motion survey of estimators, project managers, office staff, supervisors, setters, and helpers, coded task by task for current AI use and technical exposure.
caveats: OEWS publishes this occupation mix at the four-digit parent industry rather than NAICS 238340.
OEWS excludes self-employed workers, material because BLS reports 25.7% self-employment for tile and stone setters.
Task exposure is an analyst allocation, not a measured AI substitution rate, and does not assume robotic automation of field installation.

rho.rationale: Implementation should be feasible in repeatable information workflows such as takeoffs, bid drafting, scheduling, procurement follow-up, change-order documentation, invoicing, and customer communication, but fragmented data and small-firm change capacity constrain realization. Census found only 1.4% of construction firms and 1.9% of construction employment at AI-using firms in its 2024 reference period (S4), while economy-wide business AI use had risen to roughly one-fifth in the latest six-month window reported in 2026 (S5). The numeric bounds allow continued diffusion without assuming that adoption alone produces labor savings.
rho.change_evidence: A five-year cohort study of tile contractors using AI-enabled estimating, takeoff, scheduling, CRM, and accounting tools, with pre/post labor hours, override rates, rework, bid hit rate, and realized headcount avoidance by workflow.
caveats: Construction-sector AI adoption is broader than tile contracting and the 2024 Census measure used a narrower question than the 2026 measure.
Current adoption does not directly measure the five-year share of exposed labor opportunity implemented.
Low-quality plans, site variation, fragmented software, review requirements, and workforce training may limit realized savings.

e.rationale: The NAICS definition is already a contractor service population and includes new work, alterations, maintenance, and repairs (S1). Most firms in the injected EBITDA band should serve external customers, but eligibility requires a repeatable outsourced-service base; direct-to-consumer operators dependent on isolated household projects and firms with excessive owner dependence are excluded. The active market for small, locally owned construction businesses, including flooring and finishing specialties, supports meaningful eligibility but does not identify the NAICS-specific share (S8).
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of the 39 injected band candidates using customer concentration, repeat-revenue history, general-contractor relationship tenure, owner dependence, licensing, financial quality, and service-line classification.
caveats: No source measures lens eligibility among the injected LMM-band firms.
Repeat work may come through contractor relationships even when each underlying construction project is nonrecurring.
The injected firm count is an estimate from broader datasets and may include firms whose economics are not representative of transferable tile contractors.

s5.bridge: FMI surveyed nearly 300 US engineering and construction leaders and found 38% of owners planned to exit within five years; MNP/Ipsos surveyed 191 Canadian construction owners and found 30% expected an ownership transition within five years. I apply a substantial haircut to these intentions for plans that do not execute, closures, gifts or restructurings without a qualifying control transfer, and transferable-quality failures. This likely biases the estimate downward if management and employee buyouts frequently transfer control, but upward if survey respondents are older or more succession-focused than tile contractors.
s5.rationale: US engineering and construction succession evidence shows a large stated exit cohort but weak preparedness: half of the 38% planning an exit lacked an ownership transition plan (S6). The Canadian construction survey provides a separate 30% five-year transition reference and reports only 29% with a formal plan (S7). BizBuySell documents 3,142 reported building-and-construction sales in its analyzed sample and a 207-day median time on market, confirming an active transfer channel without supplying a firm-population denominator (S8).
s5.change_evidence: NAICS 238340 owner-age and succession-plan microdata linked to business-broker, SBA 7(a), private-deal, ESOP, and management-buyout records, with closures and non-control events removed and five-year outcomes tracked.
caveats: FMI covers the broader US engineering and construction sector, and respondent selection may favor established firms.
The MNP sample is Canadian and therefore differs in financing, taxes, and ownership-transfer institutions.
Stated exit intent is not a completed qualifying transfer, and broker-reported sales are not a complete market census.

q.rationale: On firm-fixed-price work, a contractor's price is not adjusted for its realized cost experience and the contractor bears the resulting profit or loss, so implemented savings can be retained during an awarded job (S10). Retention decays as jobs are rebid, general contractors compare subcontract quotes, and customers negotiate; cost-plus or time-and-material work passes savings through more directly. The market is populated mainly by small local construction-service firms and competitive sale values vary with performance and owner involvement (S8), supporting neither full retention nor immediate full pass-through.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level data from tile contractors separating lump-sum, unit-price, time-and-material, and cost-plus revenue, joined to bid history, renewal pricing, gross margin, customer concentration, win rate, and realized AI savings over five years.
caveats: No source reports the contract-type mix or savings pass-through for NAICS 238340.
The federal fixed-price rule explains contract economics but is not evidence of private tile-contract prevalence.
Retention depends on local competition, backlog, relationship quality, and whether savings improve speed or quality rather than only cost.

d5.bridge: BLS projects tile and stone setter employment, not constant-price and constant-quality service quantity. I translate the 10.1% 2024-to-2034 occupational employment increase into an approximately five-year compounded midpoint and center the demand ratio near 1.05. Employment can overstate quantity growth if productivity falls or understate it if productivity improves; the occupation also includes workers outside NAICS 238340, so the net direction is uncertain.
d5.rationale: BLS projects tile and stone setter employment from 52,600 in 2024 to 58,000 in 2034, a 10.1% increase, and states that new-home construction and renovation are the primary demand sources while tile and stone remain common in bathrooms, restaurants, and other buildings (S9). The five-year midpoint is a reasonable demand anchor, widened for construction cyclicality and the distinction between labor and service quantity.
d5.change_evidence: A NAICS 238340 real-output forecast combining Census construction put-in-place by end market, renovation spending, square footage of tile installed, material mix, project pipeline, pricing deflators, and contractor productivity through 2031.
caveats: Occupational employment is not industry output and includes tile setters employed outside the target NAICS.
The BLS projection begins in 2024 and spans a full decade, while this packet uses a five-year horizon from 2026.
Housing cycles, interest rates, renovation activity, and substitution toward resilient flooring can materially alter tile demand.

o.rationale: The service basket is an accountable, site-specific physical installation activity. O*NET tasks require aligning, cutting, setting, grouting, preparing surfaces, and handling materials (S3), while BLS describes physically demanding work requiring precision, strength, stamina, and work inside customer buildings (S9). Software can improve planning and administration but is unlikely to eliminate the responsible contractor for most year-five quantity; limited leakage reflects DIY, prefabrication, material substitution, and possible mechanized assistance.
o.change_evidence: Field trials and adoption data for robotic layout, cutting, mortar application, tile placement, modular bathroom systems, DIY channels, and direct-to-owner software, measuring the share of completed square footage delivered without a licensed or accountable tile contractor.
caveats: The estimate concerns operator necessity, not the share of tasks performed manually.
Licensing and accountability rules vary by state and project type.
Unexpected advances in field robotics or modular construction could reduce operator-required quantity more quickly.

narrative.executive_view: Tile and terrazzo contracting remains a site-bound skilled trade with a modest but practical AI opportunity in the information layer around each job. The operating case depends on standardizing estimating, scheduling, procurement, documentation, and customer workflows across repeat contractor relationships while protecting field quality and local reputation.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can assist plan reading, quantity takeoffs, bid and change-order drafting, schedule coordination, purchasing follow-up, customer communications, invoicing, and knowledge retrieval. It is not a credible substitute for the core work of preparing uneven sites, selecting and cutting material, laying patterns, setting tile, grouting, finishing, and accepting installation liability.
narrative.value_capture: Awarded fixed-price jobs can retain cost savings until completion, especially when faster estimating raises throughput or better coordination prevents rework. Benefits are progressively shared through competitive bids and negotiated repeat work; cost-plus and hourly arrangements expose savings sooner. Quality, schedule reliability, and responsiveness may preserve more value than a pure labor-cost reduction.
narrative.firm_availability: The broader construction succession surveys indicate substantial owner exit intent, but planned exits are not equivalent to completed control transfers. A viable buyer pool should focus on firms with durable general-contractor or builder relationships, documented estimating and project controls, a foreman layer, clean financials, and limited dependence on the selling owner's craft or sales relationships.
narrative.demand_durability: Tile and stone remain common in bathrooms, restaurants, and other buildings, with renovation and new construction supporting the service. The work is physically embedded in customer sites and failures create visible quality and water-intrusion consequences, preserving the need for an accountable operator even as the office layer becomes more automated.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The main risks are housing and renovation cyclicality, strong local price competition, installer scarcity, rework and warranty exposure, fragmented project data, and owner dependence. Evidence is weakest at the six-digit industry level: occupation mix comes from a parent industry, succession surveys cover broad construction populations, and no source directly measures AI task savings or commercial pass-through for tile contractors.
narrative.principal_driver: Repeatable estimating and coordination workflows can be improved while the underlying site-installed service remains difficult to displace.
narrative.principal_weakness: Most wage dollars are tied to physical craft labor, and the pool of transferable firms with clean systems and low owner dependence may be small.

sources.title: 2022 North American Industry Classification System Manual: NAICS 238340 Tile and Terrazzo Contractors
sources.supports: Defines the industry as job-site setting and installation of ceramic tile, interior stone, mosaic, and terrazzo, including new work, additions, alterations, maintenance, and repairs; identifies excluded adjacent activities.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 238300 Building Finishing Contractors
sources.supports: Provides parent-industry employment shares and mean wages by occupation group, including construction 70.96%, office support 8.59%, management 5.32%, business and financial operations 4.63%, and sales 2.94%.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Tile and Stone Setters, 47-2044.00
sources.supports: Lists the occupation's physical installation tasks and information tasks, including layout, cutting, mortar application, blueprint study, estimating, ordering, scheduling-related activities, and customer assistance.

sources.title: Tracking Firm Use of AI in Real Time: A Snapshot from the Business Trends and Outlook Survey
sources.supports: Reports 1.4% firm-weighted current AI use in construction and 1.9% of construction workers at AI-using firms in the 2024 reference period, plus workflow, training, and applicability constraints.

sources.title: Large Firms With at Least 20 Employees Biggest AI Users
sources.supports: Reports economy-wide business AI usage at roughly one-fifth during Census BTOS data collected from December 2025 through May 2026, while documenting a broader business-function question.

sources.title: FMI Releases 2024 Study on Ownership Transfer and Management Succession
sources.supports: Reports a survey of nearly 300 US engineering and construction leaders: 38% planned to exit within five years, half of them lacked an ownership transition plan, and intended transfer modes included employee and family transactions.

sources.title: Preparing for the Next Chapter in Construction: 2025 MNP Report
sources.supports: Reports an Ipsos survey of 191 Canadian construction owners: 30% expected to transition ownership within five years, 13% were currently transitioning, and 29% had a formal transition plan.

sources.title: Construction Business Valuation Multiples and Financial Benchmarks
sources.supports: Documents reported sales of small, locally owned construction businesses, including flooring and finishing specialties; the analyzed set includes 3,142 sold listings and a 207-day median time on market.

sources.title: Occupational Outlook Handbook: Flooring Installers and Tile and Stone Setters
sources.supports: Describes duties, physical requirements, work settings, training, self-employment, and demand drivers; projects tile and stone setter employment from 52,600 in 2024 to 58,000 in 2034.

sources.title: Federal Acquisition Regulation Subpart 16.2: Fixed-Price Contracts
sources.supports: Defines firm-fixed-price economics: price is not adjusted for the contractor's cost experience, and the contractor bears cost responsibility and resulting profit or loss.
